one.zh: 违令(contravention)程序:父亲指母亲自 2025 年 9 月 5 日终局命令后扣留两名女儿不与其相处。法院认定母亲就 10 月 4 日、11 月 7 日两次故意不遵令、且无合理理由,作出违令宣告,责令其就 10 岁幼女遵令签订两年保证书(bond);但因长女已 14 岁,不强制就其签保证书,亦不认为补时(make-up time)符合子女最佳利益。
one.en: Contravention proceedings: the father alleged the mother withheld their two daughters from spending time with him since the final orders of 5 September 2025. The court found the mother intentionally failed to comply on 4 October and 7 November 2025 without reasonable excuse, declared the contraventions, and required her to enter a two-year bond conditioned on complying with the orders for the 10-year-old; but, the elder daughter being 14, it would not require a bond for her, and was not satisfied make-up time was in the children's best interests.

issue.zh: 母亲是否故意不遵守养育命令而构成违令?其是否就此具备合理理由(reasonable excuse)?若构成无合理理由之违令,应采取何种制裁——保证书、补时抑或其他?
issue.en: Whether the mother intentionally failed to comply with the parenting orders (contravention); whether she had a reasonable excuse; and, if contravention without reasonable excuse is found, what sanction follows — a bond, make-up time, or other.

subseq.zh: 母亲须于命令后 7 日内到堪培拉登记处、就 2016 年出生的幼女 Z 遵守 Gill 法官 2025 年 9 月 5 日命令,签订为期两年、无担保/保证人的保证书,直至该命令被撤销、中止或变更为止;就长女 Y 不强制签保证书;费用问题保留;2026 年 3 月 24 日修订违令申请其余部分被驳回。
subseq.en: The mother is to attend the Canberra Registry within 7 days and enter a two-year bond, without surety or security, conditioned on complying with Gill J's orders of 5 September 2025 in respect of the child Z (born 2016), unless and until those orders are discharged, suspended or varied; no bond is required for the elder child Y; costs were reserved; the Amended Application – Contravention filed 24 March 2026 was otherwise dismissed.

summary.catchwords: FAMILY LAW – CONTRAVENTION – PARENTING – Where the Father alleges that, since the making of the final orders on 5 September 2025, the Mother has not complied with the “spend time” orders as sought by her – Where the Mother’s case was that she had not contravened the orders or, in the alternative, that she had a reasonable excuse for not complying with the orders – Where children (aged 14 and 10) have now not spent time with the Father in accordance with orders since March 2025 – Where Court is satisfied the Mother intentionally failed to comply with the relevant orders – Where no reasonable excuse established – Where Mother’s evidence as to the children’s reluctance to spend time with the Father is insufficiently particularised – Where the Mother gave evidence that she relies upon the views of the older child in relation to whether that child and the younger child should be made available for time with the Father – Where declaration made that the Mother contravened the orders without reasonable excuse – Where the Mother is required to enter into a two-year bond without surety or security on condition that she complies with the orders in respect of the 10 year old child – Where the Court is not satisfied that make-up time is in the children’s best interests.

hint.en: The whole case as a story

html.zh:
这是一宗违令(contravention)程序。父亲 Mr Jaskolski 指母亲 Ms Beiler 自 Gill 法官 2025 年 9 月 5 日作出终局养育命令后,扣留两名女儿 Y(14 岁)与 Z(10 岁)不与其相处;长子 X(16 岁)随父生活,非本程序对象。值得注意的是,该终局命令的「相处」安排,大体正是母亲当时所主张的方案。

父亲指控两次违令:2025 年 10 月 4 日(女儿应赴假期相处)与 11 月 7 日(应于其家乡度学期相处)。母亲先否认,主张已采取一切合理步骤遵令;退而主张具合理理由——谓 2025 年 3 月一连串事件(围绕一匹马「Horse 33」的纠纷)后,女儿拒绝前往,威胁若被强迫便离家出走,她担心女儿在父亲照料下的安全。庭审中,父亲明智地撤回了将女儿改判随父生活的请求,只求母亲遵令,并接受以保证书与补时了结。

Behrens 法官的认定:就 10 月 4 日,母亲提前一日经律师告知不会交付子女、当日带子女去看马展、未作任何劝说尝试,系故意不遵令;就 11 月 7 日,母亲不送子女上学、又拒绝另行安排交接,亦属故意不遵令。法官不接受母亲的合理理由:无可靠证据证明女儿确曾威胁出走或母亲真信其会出走,亦无证据显示 3 月事件给母亲以担心女儿安全的合理基础;且「子女不愿前往」本身不构成故意违令的合理理由。尤其令人忧虑的是,母亲承认其「完全依赖 14 岁长女 Y 的看法」来决定包括 10 岁幼女 Z 在内的相处事宜——法官认为这是对其亲职责任的放弃。

制裁:法院认定母亲无合理理由违令,依第 70NBF 条责令其就幼女 Z 遵令签订两年保证书(无担保/保证人)——因子女最佳利益、违令之严重性(终局命令新作不久、相处安排大体如其所求)而属适当;但鉴于长女 Y 已 14 岁、两年后将 16 岁,为不阻碍其随年龄增长而应有的弹性,不强制就 Y 签保证书。法院亦不认为补时符合 Y、Z 的最佳利益,予以拒绝。
html.en:
This is a contravention proceeding. The father, Mr Jaskolski, alleged that the mother, Ms Beiler, withheld their daughters Y (14) and Z (10) from spending time with him after Gill J's final parenting orders of 5 September 2025; the eldest, X (16), lives with the father and is not the subject of these proceedings. Notably, the 'spend time' arrangements in the final orders were broadly what the mother herself had sought.

The father alleged two contraventions: 4 October 2025 (when the daughters were due to travel for holiday time) and 7 November 2025 (when they were due to spend school-term time with him in their hometown). The mother first denied breach, saying she took all reasonable steps; alternatively she claimed a reasonable excuse — that after a series of events in March 2025 (a dispute over a horse, 'Horse 33') the girls refused to go, threatened to run away if compelled, and she feared for their safety in the father's care. At hearing the father sensibly withdrew his application to change the girls' residence, seeking only compliance, and was content with a bond and make-up time.

Behrens J's findings: on 4 October, the mother informed the father's lawyers the day before that she would not produce the children, took them to a horse show that day, and made no attempt to persuade them — an intentional failure to comply; on 7 November, she did not send them to school and refused to arrange an alternate changeover — again intentional. The judge rejected her reasonable-excuse case: there was no reliable evidence the girls threatened to run away or that she genuinely believed they would, nor that the March events gave her a reasonable basis to fear for their safety; and a child not wanting to go is not itself a reasonable excuse. Most troubling, the mother admitted she 'relies on Y's view of the entire situation' — including decisions about the 10-year-old Z — which the judge held was an abdication of her parental responsibility.

Sanction: having found contravention without reasonable excuse, the court required the mother under s 70NBF to enter a two-year bond (without surety or security) conditioned on complying with the orders for Z — appropriate given the children's best interests and the seriousness of the contraventions (the final orders being recent and broadly as she had sought); but, Y being 14 and turning 16 within two years, the court would not require a bond for Y so as not to impede the flexibility appropriate as young people approach adulthood. The court was also not satisfied make-up time was in Y's and Z's best interests, and refused it.

html.zh:
性质:违令程序(Pt VII Div 13A),父亲为申请人、母亲为答辩人,由 Behrens 法官于 2026 年 4 月 17 日在堪培拉(地处 Sydney 登记区)裁定。

时间线:2024 年 10-11 月、2025 年 1 月相处即已出现不遵令 → 2025-09-05 Gill 法官作出终局命令(相处安排大体如母亲所求)→ 2025-10-04、11-07 两次相处未发生 → 2025-12-16 父亲首次提出违令申请(并求改判随父)→ 2026-03-24 修订违令申请(增列补时、罚款、保证书)→ 庭审中父亲撤回改判请求 → 2026-04-17 本裁定。其中四项关于电话联络的指控,法院认定母亲无须答辩。
html.en:
Nature: a contravention proceeding (Pt VII Div 13A), father as applicant and mother as respondent, decided by Behrens J at the Canberra Registry on 17 April 2026.

Timeline: non-compliance already occurred in Oct–Nov 2024 and Jan 2025 → 5 Sep 2025, Gill J made the final orders (spend-time arrangements broadly as the mother sought) → time did not occur on 4 Oct and 7 Nov 2025 → 16 Dec 2025, the father first filed a contravention application (also seeking a change of residence) → 24 Mar 2026, the Amended Application added make-up time, a fine and a bond → at hearing the father withdrew the residence application → 17 Apr 2026, this decision. The court found the mother had no case to answer on four counts relating to telephone communication.

框架
违令的判定与制裁集中于《家庭法法》第 VII 部第 13A 分部:第 70NAC 条——「故意不遵守」或「未作合理遵守尝试」方构成违令;第 70NAD 条——合理理由(含为保护健康/安全)的认定,举证责任在违令方;第 70NBF 条——责令签保证书的权力及须考量的因素(对子女的影响、违令之严重性);第 70NCA 条——保证书的内容;第 70NBB 条——补时养育命令;第 70NAB 条——本分部之目的(支持遵令、阻吓违令、维护法院权威等)。第 60CA 条——补时属养育命令,子女最佳利益为首要。程序上:第 102NN 条容许采纳前判决之认定;第 67ZBE 条为向警方/政府部门调取材料的依据。

§ s70NAC ›

§ s70NBB ›
§ s70NCA ›
§ s70NAB ›
§ s60CA ›
§ s102NN ›
§ s67ZBE ›
Framework
The finding and sanctioning of contravention sit in Pt VII Div 13A of the Family Law Act: s 70NAC — a person contravenes only by 'intentionally' failing to comply or making 'no reasonable attempt'; s 70NAD — reasonable excuse (including to protect health/safety), the onus on the contravenor; s 70NBF — the power to require a bond and the factors to consider (effect on a child, seriousness of the contravention); s 70NCA — the content of bonds; s 70NBB — a make-up-time parenting order; s 70NAB — the objects of the Division (supporting compliance, deterring non-compliance, upholding the court's authority). Section 60CA — make-up time is a parenting order, the child's best interests paramount. Procedurally: s 102NN allowed adopting findings from the earlier judgment; s 67ZBE was the basis for obtaining material from police and a government department.

html.zh: 本判决未援引判例。法官径直适用《家庭法法》第 VII 部第 13A 分部的违令制度——故意不遵令的认定(第 70NAC 条)、合理理由的检验(第 70NAD 条),以及保证书与补时的制裁条文(第 70NBF、70NBB、70NCA 条),并依第 102NN 条采纳了 Gill 法官此前判决中的相关事实认定。
html.en: This judgment cites no case law. The judge applied the Pt VII Div 13A contravention regime of the Family Law Act directly — the test for intentional non-compliance (s 70NAC), the reasonable-excuse test (s 70NAD), and the sanction provisions for bonds and make-up time (ss 70NBF, 70NBB, 70NCA) — and adopted, under s 102NN, relevant findings of fact from Gill J's earlier judgment.

html.zh:
1. 违令的检验 —— 依第 70NAC(1)(a) 条,须证明违令方「故意不遵守」或「未作合理遵守尝试」;须按盖然性优势满足,举证责任在父亲。

2. 10 月 4 日——故意不遵令 —— 母亲提前一日经律师告知不交付子女、当日带子女去看(早已计划的)马展、未作任何劝说或将子女送车的尝试,且对父亲到场询问置之不理。法院「轻松满足」其系故意不遵令——并非「已尝试而无法遵守」。

3. 10 月 4 日——无合理理由 —— 合理理由举证在母亲(第 70NADA 条),保护健康/安全之例外见第 70NAD(3) 条。母亲所称女儿威胁出走缺乏情境与细节、且无证据显示 Z 曾威胁;法院不信女儿确曾威胁、亦不信母亲真信其会出走;3 月事件不构成对女儿安全的合理担忧基础;而「子女不愿前往」本身不是故意违令的合理理由。

4. 放弃亲职责任 —— 母亲承认「完全依赖长女 Y 的看法」决定相处事宜,甚至以 14 岁 Y 的看法决定 10 岁 Z 是否相处;又在 1 月不为女儿打包行李致相处落空。法院认为这是对亲职责任的放弃,且将责任转嫁于 Y。综合各点,法院无法确信母亲真心支持子女与父亲的关系、或愿采取合理步骤促成相处。

5. 11 月 7 日——故意不遵令、无合理理由 —— 母亲提前经律师告知不交付、不送子女上学、又拒绝另行安排交接;前述认定同样适用。且此次相处在子女家乡进行,「在偏远地可能出走」之忧不能成立;咖啡馆冲突亦不足以构成拒交子女的合理理由(况她事前已表明不交付)。

6. 制裁——保证书 —— 依第 70NBF、70NAB 条,签保证书可支持遵令、阻吓违令、促进子女最佳利益。鉴于违令之严重(终局命令新作不久、相处大体如母所求、判决理由已说明相处符合子女利益),纵无前科,签两年保证书仍属适当;唯就长女 Y 不强制签,以免阻碍其随年龄增长应有的弹性;因证据有限且恐影响子女,不要求提供担保/保证人。

7. 制裁——补时 —— 补时属养育命令,子女最佳利益为首要(第 60CA 条)。法院缺乏据以认定补时符合子女利益的证据,且忧虑在恢复常规相处之际加判补时或适得其反,故不认为补时符合 Y、Z 的最佳利益,予以拒绝。
html.en:
1. The contravention test — under s 70NAC(1)(a), it must be shown the contravenor 'intentionally' failed to comply or made 'no reasonable attempt'; proved on the balance of probabilities, the onus on the father.

2. 4 October — intentional non-compliance — the mother told the father's lawyers the day before that she would not produce the children, took them to an already-planned horse show that day, made no attempt to persuade them or get them into the car, and ignored the father's message on arrival. The court was 'comfortably satisfied' this was intentional — not a case of trying but being unable to comply.

3. 4 October — no reasonable excuse — the onus for reasonable excuse lay on the mother (s 70NADA), with the health/safety exception in s 70NAD(3). Her account that the girls threatened to run away lacked context and detail, and there was no evidence Z had so threatened; the court did not accept the girls threatened to run away or that she genuinely believed they would; the March events gave no reasonable basis to fear for their safety; and a child not wanting to go is not itself a reasonable excuse.

4. Abdication of parental responsibility — the mother admitted she 'relies on Y's view of the entire situation', even using 14-year-old Y's views to decide whether 10-year-old Z should spend time with the father; and in January she did not pack the girls' bags, defeating the time. The court held this was an abdication that transferred responsibility to Y. Overall, the court could not be satisfied she genuinely supports the children's relationship with their father or would take reasonable steps to facilitate it.

5. 7 November — intentional, no reasonable excuse — the mother told the father's lawyers in advance she would not produce the children, did not send them to school, and refused an alternate changeover; the earlier findings applied. As this time was to be in the children's hometown, fears of running away in a remote location could not apply; and a café incident was not a sufficient basis to refuse (she had already said she would not produce them).

6. Sanction — bond — under ss 70NBF and 70NAB, a bond can support compliance, deter non-compliance and promote the children's best interests. Given the seriousness of the contraventions (the final orders being recent, broadly as the mother sought, with reasons explaining why time served the children's interests), a two-year bond was appropriate despite no prior finding; but no bond was required for Y, to avoid impeding the flexibility appropriate as she approaches adulthood; and, on limited evidence and concern for the children, no security or surety was required.

7. Sanction — make-up time — make-up time is a parenting order with the children's best interests paramount (s 60CA). The court lacked evidence to find make-up time was in their interests and was concerned that ordering it alongside resuming ordinary time might be counter-productive, so it was not satisfied make-up time was in Y's and Z's best interests, and refused it.

html.zh:
裁判要旨

违令须证明「故意不遵守」或「未作合理遵守尝试」;子女表示不愿相处,本身不构成不遵令的合理理由,尤其当家长未作合理劝说、且将遵令与否系于子女(乃至另一名未成年子女)的意愿时——那是对亲职责任的放弃。

认定无合理理由之违令后,制裁须服务于第 13A 分部之目的与子女最佳利益:保证书可针对个别子女量身设定(就年幼者强制、对将近成年者保留弹性);补时属养育命令,无充分证据证明其符合子女最佳利益时,不予作出。
html.en:
Ratio

Contravention requires intentional non-compliance or no reasonable attempt to comply; a child's stated reluctance does not itself amount to a reasonable excuse, especially where the parent makes no reasonable effort to persuade and makes compliance contingent on the child's (or even another minor child's) wishes — that is an abdication of parental responsibility.

Once contravention without reasonable excuse is found, sanction must serve the objects of Div 13A and the children's best interests: a bond may be tailored child-by-child (required for a younger child, withheld for one nearing adulthood to preserve flexibility); make-up time is a parenting order and will not be made absent sufficient evidence that it is in the children's best interests.

html.zh:
Contravention · 违令
无合理理由不遵守法院命令的行为(Contravention: failing to comply with a court order without reasonable excuse).
Reasonable excuse · 合理理由
可免责的不遵令事由,如为保护健康或安全(第 70NAD 条);举证责任在违令方(Reasonable excuse: a defence to non-compliance, e.g. to protect health or safety (s 70NAD); the onus is on the contravenor).
Bond · 保证书
责令一方承诺于一定期间遵守命令的措施,违反可致罚款等后果(Bond: an undertaking to comply with orders for a set period; breach may lead to a fine and other consequences).
Make-up time · 补时
补偿因违令而失去的相处时间的养育命令(Make-up time: a parenting order compensating for time lost through contravention).
Best interests · 最佳利益
子女事项的首要考量,补时等命令均须以此为准(第 60CA 条)(Best interests: the paramount consideration for children's matters, governing orders such as make-up time (s 60CA)).
Abdication of parental responsibility · 放弃亲职责任
家长将本应自身承担的养育决定交由子女主导的情形(Abdication of parental responsibility: where a parent surrenders to the child decisions the parent should make).
Div 13A objects · 第 13A 分部目的
支持遵令、阻吓违令、维护法院权威等违令制度的立法目的(s 70NAB)(Div 13A objects: the statutory aims of the contravention regime — supporting compliance, deterring non-compliance, upholding the court's authority (s 70NAB)).
html.en:
Contravention
Failing to comply with a court order without reasonable excuse.
Reasonable excuse
A defence to non-compliance, e.g. to protect health or safety (s 70NAD); the onus is on the contravenor.
Bond
An undertaking to comply with orders for a set period; breach may lead to a fine and other consequences.
Make-up time
A parenting order compensating for time lost through contravention.
Best interests
The paramount consideration for children's matters, governing orders such as make-up time (s 60CA).
Abdication of parental responsibility
Where a parent surrenders to the child decisions the parent should make.
Div 13A objects
The statutory aims of the contravention regime — supporting compliance, deterring non-compliance, upholding the court's authority (s 70NAB).

html.zh:
REASONS FOR JUDGMENT

BEHRENS J

BACKGROUND

These are contravention proceedings in which Mr Jaskolski is the applicant and Ms Beiler is the respondent. They are the parents of X (aged 16), Y (aged 14) and Z (aged 10).

X lives with Mr Jaskolski and is not the subject of these proceedings. Y and Z live with Ms Beiler. The parents live in small rural towns, approximately nine hours’ drive apart.

The Amended Application – Contravention filed by Mr Jaskolski on 24 March 2026 (“the amended application”) broadly alleges that Ms Beiler has withheld Y and Z from spending time with their father in accordance with final parenting orders which were made by Gill J on 5 September 2025. Those parenting orders were made following a final hearing in parenting and property proceedings which took place over six days in October 2024 and January 2025. In the parenting proceedings, Mr Jaskolski was seeking that Y and Z live with him. Ms Beiler was seeking that Y and Z continue to live with her and spend time with Mr Jaskolski. It was agreed that X would remain living with Mr Jaskolski and spend time with Ms Beiler in accordance with his wishes. The parenting orders made by Gill J were broadly as sought by Ms Beiler.

It is uncontested that Y and Z have now not spent time with their father or brother in accordance with orders since March 2025 and, prior to that, they did not spend ordered time in November 2024 and January 2025. The amended application alleges breaches on the first two occasions that time was to occur after the making of the final orders – namely, on 4 October 2025 (when Y and Z were due to travel to spend holiday time with Mr Jaskolski) and on 7 November 2025 (when they were due to spend school term time with Mr Jaskolski in their hometown).

Notwithstanding that, after judgment was reserved, time was not occurring in accordance with interim orders, neither party sought to reopen the proceedings. Ms Beiler has never complied with the “spend time” aspects of the final orders which were broadly made in the terms that she continued to seek at delivery of judgment and in the same terms as the interim orders with which she was not complying after March 2025.

Upon the making of the final orders, Mr Jaskolski’s lawyer wrote to Ms Beiler’s lawyer, communicating the expectation that Y and Z would now be provided for time with Mr Jaskolski in accordance with these orders, and that Mr Jaskolski was to spend holiday time with the children commencing 4 October 2025. On 3 October 2025, Ms Beiler’s lawyers responded that the holiday time would not occur and, ultimately, the girls did not spend this time with their father. Time also did not occur in accordance with the orders in November 2025. An Application – Contravention in respect of the time the children were to spend with their father in October and November 2025 was first filed by Mr Jaskolski on 16 December 2025 (“the first contravention application”).

By the first contravention application, Mr Jaskolski was seeking a variation of the final orders such that Y and Z would move to live with him. By the amended application, Mr Jaskolski continued to seek that variation but, in the alternative, he sought orders for make-up time. He also sought the imposition of a fine, and that Ms Beiler enter into a bond. During closing submissions, the application for a variation to the final orders was sensibly withdrawn and counsel for Mr Jaskolski indicated that Mr Jaskolski simply wanted Ms Beiler to comply with the orders and would be content with a bond and an order for make-up time.

When they were formally put to her, Ms Beiler denied each of what were initially seven counts of contravening the final orders. I found that she had no case to answer in respect of the first four counts (which related to telephone communication). Ms Beiler sought the dismissal of the amended application. Initially, she was seeking orders for Mr Jaskolski, Y and Z to attend family therapy, but that application was sensibly withdrawn in circumstances where there was no evidence before me about the availability, cost or suitability of family therapy and where, in any event, there are doubts about my power to make such an order in contravention proceedings.

Ms Beiler’s case was expressed in the alternative, at least initially. First, it was said that she had not contravened the relevant orders because she had taken all reasonable steps to comply. Alternatively, it was said that she had a reasonable excuse for failing to comply with the relevant orders. The essence of Ms Beiler’s case was that, following and in the context of events which occurred in March 2025 (being the last occasion time in accordance with the orders occurred), the children were refusing to attend time, she had unsuccessfully encouraged the children to attend, and they had threatened to run away if they were compelled to attend time with their father. These matters were said to go both to her reasonable steps to comply and, in the alternative, to reasonable excuse.

On behalf of Mr Jaskolski, it was said that Ms Beiler’s conduct “forms part of a broader and longstanding pattern of non-compliance” which pre-dated the making of the final orders and that her failure to comply following the making of final orders “cannot be viewed in isolation” (Mr Jaskolski’s Case Outline filed 7 April 2026, paragraph 11).

Mr Jaskolski gave evidence that he believed that Ms Beiler was withholding Y and Z because X remained living with Mr Jaskolski and is not spending time with Ms Beiler. Counsel for Mr Jaskolski made the oral submissions that Ms Beiler was “alienating” the girls from their father (although it was denied that they had in fact expressed a reluctance to spend time with their father) and that Ms Beiler’s evidence regarding Y’s concerns about spending time with her father was “make believe”. This is a complex matter involving entrenched parental conflict which cannot be reduced to these kinds of terms.

For the reasons set out below, I am satisfied that Ms Beiler has contravened Orders 5(a) and 5(c) as alleged. I am not satisfied that she had a reasonable excuse for doing so. I require her to enter a bond with the condition that she comply with the orders in respect of Z for a period of two years. Ms Beiler remains obliged to comply with the parenting orders in respect of Y but, given Y’s age, I am not prepared to require Ms Beiler to enter a bond in respect of Y. I am not satisfied that it is in the children’s best interests to order “make up time”.

MATERIAL RELIED UPON

Mr Jaskolski relied on his affidavits filed 16 December 2025 and 2 April 2026. I received those affidavits into evidence with their annexures. Those annexures included, in relation to the previous proceedings, the Reasons for Judgment of Gill J dated 5 September 2025 (“Reasons for Judgment”), the Updated Family Report of Court Child Expert Ms C dated 11 December 2024 (“the Updated Family Report”), and the Child Impact Report of Court Child Expert Ms C dated 11 May 2023 (“the Child Impact Report”). Tendered in his case was an email exchange between the parents in the lead up to time occurring between 28 February and 10 March 2025.

Ms Beiler relied on her affidavit filed 27 March 2026, which I received into evidence with its annexure. She also relied on an affidavit of her partner, Mr R filed on 27 March 2026. Affidavits of Ms T and Ms M were not relied upon.

I have read and considered all the evidence, including the oral evidence which was given under cross examination by each of the parents and by Mr R.

Material had been produced by the Department of Communities and Justice and by NSW Police pursuant to an order under s 67ZBE of the Family Law Act 1975 (Cth) (“the Act”) – indeed, the hearing of the matter had been delayed to allow time for that material to be returned – but neither party tendered the material, and so I assume that it did not assist either party’s case.

Both parties filed Case Outlines. I have read and considered the written submissions contained in the Case Outline filed on behalf of Ms Beiler. I have read the Case Outline filed on behalf of Mr Jaskolski, which was settled by his counsel. The latter Case Outline contained a detailed chronology. The chronology contained no references to the evidential sources for the entries and contained many entries which were not supported by evidence that would be before me. I have ignored that chronology. I considered the closing oral submissions made by both counsel.

SOME BACKGROUND

Seven months ago, Gill J determined that it was in the interests of Y and Z to spend time with their father broadly in accordance with the proposals of their mother. That has never occurred. His Honour’s Reasons for Judgment were in evidence in these proceedings, and I am entitled to adopt any finding made by his Honour (s 102NN(3)(b) of the Act).

X’s move to live with Mr Jaskolski

X is now 16 and is not the subject of these contravention proceedings. The final orders provide that X is to live with Mr Jaskolski and spend time and communicate with Ms Beiler “in accordance with his wishes”. Mr Jaskolski contends that the background to that outcome is relevant to my assessment of Ms Beiler’s conduct in this matter.

In October 2024, X was 14 and a half. Justice Gill found at [183]–[184] that:

October 2024 marked a sharp breakdown of parenting arrangements, with [X]. On 12 October, shortly before the commencement of the trial on 21 October, [X] refused to return from the care of the husband to the wife’s care. It appears that [X] had planned to remain with the husband following the holiday as he had discreetly removed some of his belonging and taken them with him to his father’s house.

184 The wife sought a recovery order, which was opposed by the husband.

His Honour continued at [199]–[201]:

In response to the wife’s application, on 22 October 2024 I caused [X] to attend upon a Court Child Expert, to whom [X] expressed strongly that he would not return to his mother, and expressed that he did not feel safe to return to her.

In response to the interim application for the return of [X], on 23 October 2024 I made interim orders that provided for [X] to live with the husband ...

The husband was due to spend time with [Y] and [Z] on 15 November 2024. The wife messaged the husband the day prior, saying that the girls would not be seeing him. The wife explained the withholding to the report writer on the basis that she “lost confidence in both the existing Court orders and the willingness to enforce them.”

Other relevant findings of Gill J

Thus, Ms Beiler did not provide Y or Z for ordered time with their father in November 2024.

I adopt the following further relevant findings:

At [206]–[207] of the Reasons for Judgment:

The husband was due to collect the girls on 10 January 2025. The wife and [Mr R] attended with the girls for handover, but without any luggage for the girls. [Y] said to the husband that she and [Z] wanted to return early for [Z’s] birthday. The wife and [Mr R] intervened, effectively insisting that the husband there and then accede to the request for an early return (on the basis that this accorded with the wife’s understanding of what the girls wanted). A heated argument took place in the presence of the children between the adults.

207 The girls did not go with the husband. ...

On this occasion, “the wife was undermining the girls’ time with the husband, in a manner inconsistent with her obligations pursuant to the orders” (at [207] of the Reasons for Judgment).

This occurred after the release of the Family Report, which had “cautioned against the wife preventing the girls spending time with the husband” (at [208] of the Reasons for Judgment).

“... While in this instance there was not a direct withholding, the wife’s failure to encourage and support the girls’ time with X and the husband in accordance with the orders failed to take heed of the caution expressed by the report writer” (Reasons for Judgment at [208]).

In relation to Y’s and Z’s views, his Honour found at [215]–[216] that:

[Y] and [Z] expressed highly positive views of the wife, consistent with both a more positive relationship with the wife [than [X]], but also consistent with each harbouring concerns as to the wife’s reaction.

[Y] and [Z] expressed strong views across all of the assessments that they want to live with the wife ...

While his Honour did not make any findings about Y’s and Z’s views in relation to spending time with Mr Jaskolski (presumably because both parents were seeking at least that this occur), the Updated Family Report records that both wanted to spend time with Mr Jaskolski in accordance with the arrangements then in place (which were broadly the same as those ultimately ordered).

The events of March 2025

On behalf of Ms Beiler, it is said that the events of early March 2025 and the behaviour to which the children were exposed are the context in which the children will not spend time with Mr Jaskolski. Ms Beiler gave evidence that those events “caused me serious concern for [Y’s and Z’s] emotional and physical safety”.

In March 2025, judgment was reserved. The proceedings were not re-opened. There are therefore no relevant findings of Gill J in relation to those events.

Mr Jaskolski’s first affidavit (filed with the first contravention application) made no reference to those events. Ms Beiler’s affidavit dealt in detail with those events. In his affidavit in reply, Mr Jaskolski then gave evidence about what he says occurred.

I accept the submission of counsel for Ms Beiler that the events of March 2025 do neither party any credit and involved a significant lack of child focus by both parents. The events involved a dispute over a horse (“[Horse 33]”) which Mr Jaskolski had brought with him, along with another horse, when he travelled with X to Y’s and Z’s hometown to spend time with them there for 10 days during the school term.

The accounts given by the parents and Mr R differ in significant respects and I am able and, for the purposes of this application, need only to make very limited findings. Horse 33 had been given by Mr Jaskolski to X, and I accept Mr Jaskolski’s evidence that X rode Horse 33 daily and loved his horse. On 1 March 2025, being the second day of Y’s and Z’s time with Mr Jaskolski, Horse 33 and the other horse escaped and entered Ms Beiler’s property. At the time, Mr Jaskolski and X apparently believed that Ms Beiler was deliberately withholding Horse 33. Mr Jaskolski undertook various unhelpful and public steps (in a small community) to try and recover Horse 33. X was very distressed at the loss of his horse. Mr Jaskolski drove X to Ms Beiler’s property so that he could speak with his mother and try to recover Horse 33. Y and Z remained at the house where they were staying with Mr Jaskolski. Ms Beiler’s parents, but not Ms Beiler and Mr R, were at Ms Beiler’s residence. There was an encounter between X and the maternal grandparents which I accept likely involved X using foul language towards them. Ms Beiler did not return X’s horse, and she gave evidence that Horse 33 remains on her property and that she is not prepared to return Horse 33. She was not asked why. The other horse was returned.

It is not contested that, in a subsequent telephone call to his mother, X used foul language in the face of her refusal to return Horse 33. It is not contested that the police were telephoned following X’s interaction with his grandparents, and that the police attended the house where Mr Jaskolski and X were staying with Y and Z, and spoke with X. The police took no further action against X. Mr Jaskolski provided an audio recording of X using foul language to another person in the community, who played the recording to Ms Beiler. Mr Jaskolski says he regrets doing so, but was trying to recover the horse for X, who was in great distress.

So far as what occurred for Y and Z at this time, the parents’ accounts differ profoundly. At the time of the March 2025 events, the girls were staying with their father and X. In summary, Mr Jaskolski gives evidence that in relation to the March 2025 events, the girls were concerned for X and his obvious distress in relation to Horse 33, were distressed themselves and sought to comfort X. Mr Jaskolski deposes to happy times with the girls and X at the Town G show and with Z at the park. He denies the numerous accusations that Ms Beiler makes about that time – including that he removed Y’s phone, that X was swearing and punching walls and a range of other things.

Ms Beiler’s evidence about what she says the girls experienced while in the care of Mr Jaskolski and with X is of poor quality, in that it does not particularise how she is aware of this information, notwithstanding that for most of this time, she was not present. She refers vaguely to having been told about Mr Jaskolski’s behaviour during this period by a neighbour, by Mr R or by Y, but provides no details of any such conversations. I attach no weight to that evidence and, appropriately, Mr Jaskolski was not cross examined about it.

Under cross examination, Ms Beiler accepted that she had received a text message from Z which read “please return the horse to [X]”. That provides some corroboration of Mr Jaskolski’s evidence that the girls were concerned for X. Ms Beiler was evasive about the circumstances in which X used Y’s phone to ask for the return of his horse, refuting the suggestion that Y had allowed her brother to use her phone and instead claiming “she phoned me and he took the phone from her”. She also could not simply accept that X had asked for his horse back, but rather chose to focus on telling me that X “swore and yelled”. Clearly X was asking for his horse back and her refusal to accept that does her no credit.

Notwithstanding what occurred, and that they were in their hometown and very close to their home (and so could easily have returned), Y and Z spent time with Mr Jaskolski for the two weekends and the school week provided for under the final orders, and Mr Jaskolski took them to extra-curricular activities. That is more consistent with Mr Jaskolski’s evidence than the evidence of Ms Beiler.

The events of the period from 1 March 2025 were no doubt distressing for the girls. Those distressing events were caused by both parents in the context of the ongoing conflict and poor child focus. I am not persuaded that there was anything in what occurred which gave Ms Beiler any reasonable basis for concern about the girls’ safety in the care of Mr Jaskolski.

COUNTS 5 AND 6 – WITHHOLDING ON 4 OCTOBER 2025

Both counts relate to the events of 4 October 2025, when Y and Z were to be taken to changeover at a point approximately halfway between the parents’ respective homes, to spend school holiday time with their father. The contraventions are pleaded in two different ways. Count 5 alleges that Ms Beiler contravened Order 5(c) in that she “without reasonable excuse withheld the children from spending holiday time with [Mr Jaskolski]”. Count 6 alleges that Ms Beiler contravened Order 5(d) (which specifies the handover location), in that she “without reasonable excuse failed to produce/deliver the children at/to changeover at the commencement of holiday time with [Mr Jaskolski]”. This Count is misconceived in that the obligation is created by Order 5(c) (together with Order 5(b)), rather than 5(d) (which is mechanical only). I therefore deal only with Count 5.

Did Ms Beiler contravene Order 5(c)?

A person contravenes a child-related order only if that person “intentionally fails to comply with the order” or “makes no reasonable attempt to comply with the order” (s 70NAC(1)(a)). I must be satisfied on the balance of probabilities that the contravention has occurred, and the onus lies on Mr Jaskolski.

I am comfortably satisfied that Ms Beiler intentionally failed to comply with Order 5(c) on 4 October by withholding Y and Z, and that therefore she contravened that order. This is not a case where Ms Beiler tried to comply with the order but was unable to – as was implied by some of the submissions made on her behalf before the evidence was heard. She simply decided not to comply in circumstances where she says Y and Z were telling her they did not want to go.

That this is the case is made clear by the following:

(1) Ms Beiler instructed her lawyers to inform Mr Jaskolski via his lawyers on 3 October 2025 (the day before time was to occur) that she would not be making the children available for time with their father. Mr Jaskolski’s lawyers responded on the same day, advising that he would be at the changeover point at the appointed time. Mr Jaskolski attended the changeover location on 4 October, and sent a message to Ms Beiler when he arrived, asking how far away she was. Ms Beiler did not trouble to reply.

(2) Ms Beiler and Mr R gave evidence under cross examination that, on that day, they were watching a horse show with the children at Town U. Mr R agreed with the proposition put to him by counsel for Mr Jaskolski that their attendance at the show had been planned “for some time”.

(3) Ms Beiler gave no evidence that she had attempted on 4 October to get the children into the car, nor indeed that she had taken any step at all on that day to try to persuade them to attend.

Ms Beiler intentionally failed to comply with Order 5(c) on 4 October 2025.

Did Ms Beiler have a reasonable excuse for contravening the orders on 4 October 2025?

Ms Beiler bears the onus of persuading me that she has a reasonable excuse for contravening this order (s 70NADA of the Act). Sub-section 70NAD(3) sets out when a person has a reasonable excuse for contravening a child-related order:

Protection of health or safety of a person

(3) A person has a reasonable excuse for contravening a child‑related order if:

(a) the person contravened the order because the person reasonably believed that the person’s actions constituting the contravention were necessary to protect the health or safety of the person, a child or any other person; and

(b) the period of the contravention was not longer than necessary to protect the health or safety of the person, child or other person.

The section does not limit the circumstances in which a person may have a reasonable excuse for contravening a child-related order (s 70NAD(4)).

In the Case Outline filed on Ms Beiler’s behalf, the submission was made at paragraph 41 that she “says that she has a reasonable excuse in that she ought to be excused and there was a serious threat to the health and safety of the children, where she gives evidence of threats to ‘run away’ which had force given their ages”.

The relevant evidence given by Ms Beiler was as follows:

(1) “[Y] told me that if she and [Z] were made to travel to [City E] then at the first available opportunity she would take [Z] and hide in the bush until I could come and collect them”;

(2) “After discussing the situation with [Y] at length, she remained adamant that she did not want to go and that if she was made to go she would run away”;

(3) “... both girls were adamant that they would not go to [City E] to spend time with [Mr Jaskolski] and [Y] was adamant that if attempts were made to force them to do so they would run away”;

(4) Ms Beiler conceded under cross examination that it was “not necessarily [Z]” who had raised the prospect of running away, but rather “mostly [Y]”.

It can be immediately observed that:

(1) There was no context whatsoever provided for these conversations, and very limited detail of any conversation between Ms Beiler and either or both of her daughters.

(2) There was also no evidence that Z had threatened to run away.

I am not satisfied based on this evidence that either of the girls threatened to run away, nor that Ms Beiler genuinely believed that they would. I reiterate that I am also not satisfied that there was anything in the events of the week commencing 1 March 2025 which gave Ms Beiler a reasonable basis to be concerned for the safety of the Y and Z in the care of Mr Jaskolski.

To the extent that the reasonable excuse defence relies on what it is said are Y’s and Z’s views more generally, I find and determine as follows:

(1) There is no reliable evidence which enables me to conclude what those views are:

(a) If Y and Z do not want to spend time with Mr Jaskolski, that would be a very significant change from their views expressed to the Family Report writer (albeit in November 2024).

(b) Again, Ms Beiler’s affidavit evidence is insufficiently particularised.

(c) Again, there is no evidence of any weight about Z’s views.

(d) Mr Jaskolski’s evidence, while also unsatisfactory, was to the effect that the girls tell him they want to spend time with him.

(2) In any case, it is not a reasonable excuse for intentionally not complying with Court orders that children do not want to spend time with their parent in accordance with those orders. Again, there is no sufficiently particularised evidence about Ms Beiler’s attempts to persuade them, and I do not find that she did. She chose to take them to a horse show instead.

(3) Under cross examination, Ms Beiler gave evidence that leads me to conclude that she takes the view that if the children tell her they want to spend time with their father she will make that happen, but not otherwise. She gave such evidence specifically in relation to the children’s holiday time with their father, which was due to occur on the Saturday immediately following the contravention hearing. In addition, when asked by me about the events of January 2025 (when she took the girls to changeover but without their bags), Ms Beiler initially obfuscated by telling me that neither of the girls wanted to go for the whole time and wanted to negotiate with their father for an early return. When I asked again why she did not take the girls’ bags, she answered “well, because [Y] quite strongly – and [Z], but mostly [Y] – felt quite strongly that if she went, he wouldn’t bring her back”. When pressed by me as to whether she deliberately did not pack the girls’ bags so that they would not be able to spend this time with their father, Ms Beiler stated “I don’t pack their bags, they pack their bags. They refused to pack their bags” and stated that she did not tell Y and Z to pack their bags (or pack the girls’ bags herself, for that matter) because “they wanted to speak to their father about the holiday arrangements”. By behaving in this manner, she abdicated her parental responsibility.

(4) Even more troubling was her evidence to the effect that she relies on Y’s views not only in relation to what is best for Y, but also in relation to what is best for Z (who is 10). Her affidavit evidence was that:

[Y] has also expressed strong concern for the safety of her younger sister, [Z], who is aged 10 years, and has stated that she does not believe it is safe for [Z] to spend time with [Mr Jaskolski], particularly if she is not there. She has also said that she does not like [Z] speaking on the phone to [Mr Jaskolski] without her present. She says that when she watches [Mr Jaskolski] with her or speaking to her she observes him manipulating her, which she feels is unsafe and worries her. She has told me that she is deeply concerned that if [Z] were to spend time with [Mr Jaskolski] without her present, that he would simply buy her an iPad, keep her in his home, and say that she does not want to return, and that [Z] would have no way of contacting us herself. She has expressed her worry about this to me on many occasions.

Under cross examination, she frankly admitted that “I rely on [Y’s] view of the entire situation”. Again, this is an abdication of her parental responsibility but worse both because it transfers that responsibility to Y, and because the views of a 14-year-old are not an appropriate basis for making decisions not to comply with orders of this Court (particularly in respect of her 10-year-old sister).

(5) In answer to a question (in relation to Count 7) as to whether it was appropriate not to require the children to go to school in response to what she said was their reluctance to attend on the day they would enter their father’s care for school term time pursuant to the final orders, Ms Beiler also gave evidence that “I really don’t want to damage my relationship with my daughters”.

(6) Also relevant is the fact that Ms Beiler has previously not provided the children for time with Mr Jaskolski in November 2024 and January 2025 in the circumstances which are dealt with in the Reasons for Judgment, and prior to the events which occurred in March 2025.

The matters addressed above contribute to my inability to be satisfied as to any of the following:

that Ms Beiler genuinely supports the children’s relationship with their father;

that she is willing to take reasonable steps to ensure that they spend time with him;

that the withholding of the children is for the reasons she has provided, or linked in the ways she claims to the events of March 2025; and

that such withholding is reasonable.

Accordingly, I am not satisfied that Ms Beiler had a reasonable excuse for the contravention which occurred on 4 October 2025.

COUNT 7 – WITHHOLDING ON 7 NOVEMBER 2025

Time on this occasion was to occur during the school term in Y’s and Z’s hometown, to which Mr Jaskolski and X had driven for nine hours. While the orders do not specify the changeover location, it is clear that changeover is to occur on a school day (which 7 November 2025 was) by Mr Jaskolski collecting Y and Z from school; the orders provide that time is to occur “from the conclusion of school” and “to the commencement of school”.

Has Ms Beiler contravened Order 5(a)?

I am satisfied that Ms Beiler did not send Y and Z to school on 7 November for the purpose of withholding the children from spending time with their father. Not only did she not send them to school, but she did not make alternate arrangements for changeover so as to enable the time to occur, despite Mr Jaskolski requesting that she do so after he discovered that the children were not at school.

That Ms Beiler did not intend to comply with the order on 7 November was made clear by the fact that, on 5 November, she instructed her lawyers to advise Mr Jaskolski’s lawyers that the children would not be made available. She followed through on that intention by not sending the children to school, and that conduct, combined with her refusal to respond to Mr Jaskolski’s request for an alternate changeover arrangement, resulted in time not occurring. Again, these were not circumstances where Ms Beiler attempted to comply with the orders but was not able to do so by reasonable efforts.

On 7 November 2025, Ms Beiler intentionally failed to comply with Order 5(a).

Did Ms Beiler have a reasonable excuse for contravening the orders on 7 November 2025?

I am not satisfied that Ms Beiler had a reasonable excuse for contravening the orders on 7 November 2025. My findings in relation to the contravention on 4 October 2025 are apposite. In addition, time on this occasion was to be spent in their hometown. To the extent that Ms Beiler relies on concerns that the children might run away in a remote location, they could not apply in relation to this time, as it was to occur in the small country town where they live.

I accept that on 7 November, before Y and Z were due to enter Mr Jaskolski’s care, there was a distressing interaction between Mr R and Mr Jaskolski, when Mr Jaskolski arrived at a café while the children were all having lunch with Ms Beiler. I cannot make findings about the details of what occurred, other than to accept that Mr Jaskolski said words to Mr R in front of Y, which he now says he regrets. That incident is not a sufficient basis for Ms Beiler reasonably to refuse to provide the children; in any case, she had already informed Mr Jaskolski through her solicitors that she would not do so and so I cannot accept that the reason she did not provide them lies in what occurred at the café on 7 November.

ORDERS

Ultimately, and to his credit, Mr Jaskolski only sought an order that Ms Beiler enter a bond and an order for make-up time.

Having found that Ms Beiler contravened the orders without reasonable excuse, I have the power by s 70NBF(1)(a) of the Act to require her to enter a bond, with the matters dealt with in bonds set out in ss 70NCA(2), 70NCA(3) and 70NCA(4). I also have the power to make a “make-up time parenting order” (s 70NBB of the Act).

A bond?

In respect of the imposition of a bond, I am required to have regard to “the likely effects of making the order on any child, or any other person” (s 70NBF(2)(a)) and “the seriousness of the contravention” (s 70NBF(2)(b)). In assessing the seriousness of the contraventions, I am required by s 70NBF(3) to have regard to:

(a) whether a court has previously found that the respondent has contravened a child-related order without having a reasonable excuse;

(b) whether the respondent behaved in a way that showed a serious disregard of the respondent’s obligations under the child-related order ...

(c) the behaviour of any person with whom the child is to live or spend time under the child-related order ...

I also have regard to the objects of Div 13A of Pt VII which are set out in s 70NAB of the Act:

The principal objects of this Division are to meet the best interests of children to whom child‑related orders relate by:

(a) supporting compliance with child‑related orders; and

(b) resolving difficulties associated with child‑related orders that are parenting orders which have contributed to non‑compliance with such orders (including by varying or making further orders); and

(c) deterring non‑compliance with child‑related orders; and

(d) upholding the authority of the court by enforcing compliance with child‑related orders where the court considers this necessary and appropriate; and

(e) providing for sanctions for a person who contravenes a child‑related order without reasonable excuse.

I am satisfied that the making of an order which requires Ms Beiler to enter a bond will support compliance (and deter non-compliance) with the orders, consistently with the objects of Div 13A of Pt VII of the Act. I am satisfied that such an order will promote Y’s and Z’s best interests by increasing the likelihood of them spending time with and benefiting from relationships with their father and X. I find that Ms Beiler has acted in serious disregard of her obligations under the relevant orders – particularly having regard to the facts that, at the time of her contraventions, the final orders had only recently been made, the spend time orders were broadly as she had sought, and that the Reasons for Judgment made clear that spending time with their father was in Y’s and Z’s best interests, and why that is so. Notwithstanding that there has been no previous finding of contravention without reasonable excuse, I am satisfied that requiring her to enter a bond is an appropriate order to make, having regard to the seriousness of the contraventions. There is no conduct of Mr Jaskolski that leads me to conclude that the contraventions are not serious and that requiring Ms Beiler to enter a bond is not appropriate. Mr Jaskolski has diligently sought to comply with the relevant orders – including by travelling long distances in the hope that Ms Beiler would comply with the orders, despite her advising to the contrary.

I have considered whether the conditions of the bond should require compliance with the orders in respect of both Y and Z, and I have decided that they should only require compliance with the orders in respect of Z. This is because Y is now 14 and in two years’ time will be 16. Accordingly, I am concerned about the impact of the imposition of a bond on Y, because it may impede the necessary progression to flexibility in relation to parenting orders as young people progress towards adulthood. Both parents have indicated at least the possibility that they will commence new parenting proceedings – in those circumstances, it is appropriate that the bond requires compliance with the orders unless and until they are varied, suspended or discharged.

Mr Jaskolski sought that Ms Beiler provide significant security for the bond. I have limited evidence about the parents’ current financial circumstances. At the time of the final hearing, they had a relatively modest property pool of approximately $1.5 million to divide between them and Ms Beiler’s income was in the average range, and Mr Jaskolski’s income was modest. Both parents have both incurred significant legal costs. I cannot be satisfied that a requirement on Ms Beiler to provide security or surety would not have a negative impact on the children – a matter which it is relevant for me to consider. As I will explain to Ms Beiler prior to handing down these Reasons and making the orders, if she fails to comply with bond, she exposes herself to the risk of a fine and to other serious consequences. I expect that will be a sufficient deterrent to further contraventions if there is anything which can deter them.

Should I order “make-up time”?

I have considered whether to order make-up time as sought by Mr Jaskolski. Such an order is a parenting order, and Y’s and Z’s best interests are the paramount consideration (s 60CA of the Act). I am unable to make any findings which would enable me to determine what is in their best interests in terms of make-up time, other than that they have not spent time with their father in accordance with orders for more than a year, that they live nine hours’ drive from him, and that the orders already provide for them to spend half of school holidays with their father. I am concerned that ordering make-up time alongside the recommencement of ordinary time may be counter-productive to the girls’ relationship with their father. I also do not have any evidence which enables me to assess what duration and frequency of holiday time is practicable, having regard to the girls’ activities and other commitments. It is understandable that Mr Jaskolski is anxious to have additional time with Y and Z. His legitimate interests are a relevant matter, but must give way to the children’s best interests. I am not persuaded that an order for make-up time is in Y’s and Z’s best interests.
html.en:
REASONS FOR JUDGMENT

BEHRENS J

BACKGROUND

These are contravention proceedings in which Mr Jaskolski is the applicant and Ms Beiler is the respondent. They are the parents of X (aged 16), Y (aged 14) and Z (aged 10).

X lives with Mr Jaskolski and is not the subject of these proceedings. Y and Z live with Ms Beiler. The parents live in small rural towns, approximately nine hours’ drive apart.

The Amended Application – Contravention filed by Mr Jaskolski on 24 March 2026 (“the amended application”) broadly alleges that Ms Beiler has withheld Y and Z from spending time with their father in accordance with final parenting orders which were made by Gill J on 5 September 2025. Those parenting orders were made following a final hearing in parenting and property proceedings which took place over six days in October 2024 and January 2025. In the parenting proceedings, Mr Jaskolski was seeking that Y and Z live with him. Ms Beiler was seeking that Y and Z continue to live with her and spend time with Mr Jaskolski. It was agreed that X would remain living with Mr Jaskolski and spend time with Ms Beiler in accordance with his wishes. The parenting orders made by Gill J were broadly as sought by Ms Beiler.

It is uncontested that Y and Z have now not spent time with their father or brother in accordance with orders since March 2025 and, prior to that, they did not spend ordered time in November 2024 and January 2025. The amended application alleges breaches on the first two occasions that time was to occur after the making of the final orders – namely, on 4 October 2025 (when Y and Z were due to travel to spend holiday time with Mr Jaskolski) and on 7 November 2025 (when they were due to spend school term time with Mr Jaskolski in their hometown).

Notwithstanding that, after judgment was reserved, time was not occurring in accordance with interim orders, neither party sought to reopen the proceedings. Ms Beiler has never complied with the “spend time” aspects of the final orders which were broadly made in the terms that she continued to seek at delivery of judgment and in the same terms as the interim orders with which she was not complying after March 2025.

Upon the making of the final orders, Mr Jaskolski’s lawyer wrote to Ms Beiler’s lawyer, communicating the expectation that Y and Z would now be provided for time with Mr Jaskolski in accordance with these orders, and that Mr Jaskolski was to spend holiday time with the children commencing 4 October 2025. On 3 October 2025, Ms Beiler’s lawyers responded that the holiday time would not occur and, ultimately, the girls did not spend this time with their father. Time also did not occur in accordance with the orders in November 2025. An Application – Contravention in respect of the time the children were to spend with their father in October and November 2025 was first filed by Mr Jaskolski on 16 December 2025 (“the first contravention application”).

By the first contravention application, Mr Jaskolski was seeking a variation of the final orders such that Y and Z would move to live with him. By the amended application, Mr Jaskolski continued to seek that variation but, in the alternative, he sought orders for make-up time. He also sought the imposition of a fine, and that Ms Beiler enter into a bond. During closing submissions, the application for a variation to the final orders was sensibly withdrawn and counsel for Mr Jaskolski indicated that Mr Jaskolski simply wanted Ms Beiler to comply with the orders and would be content with a bond and an order for make-up time.

When they were formally put to her, Ms Beiler denied each of what were initially seven counts of contravening the final orders. I found that she had no case to answer in respect of the first four counts (which related to telephone communication). Ms Beiler sought the dismissal of the amended application. Initially, she was seeking orders for Mr Jaskolski, Y and Z to attend family therapy, but that application was sensibly withdrawn in circumstances where there was no evidence before me about the availability, cost or suitability of family therapy and where, in any event, there are doubts about my power to make such an order in contravention proceedings.

Ms Beiler’s case was expressed in the alternative, at least initially. First, it was said that she had not contravened the relevant orders because she had taken all reasonable steps to comply. Alternatively, it was said that she had a reasonable excuse for failing to comply with the relevant orders. The essence of Ms Beiler’s case was that, following and in the context of events which occurred in March 2025 (being the last occasion time in accordance with the orders occurred), the children were refusing to attend time, she had unsuccessfully encouraged the children to attend, and they had threatened to run away if they were compelled to attend time with their father. These matters were said to go both to her reasonable steps to comply and, in the alternative, to reasonable excuse.

On behalf of Mr Jaskolski, it was said that Ms Beiler’s conduct “forms part of a broader and longstanding pattern of non-compliance” which pre-dated the making of the final orders and that her failure to comply following the making of final orders “cannot be viewed in isolation” (Mr Jaskolski’s Case Outline filed 7 April 2026, paragraph 11).

Mr Jaskolski gave evidence that he believed that Ms Beiler was withholding Y and Z because X remained living with Mr Jaskolski and is not spending time with Ms Beiler. Counsel for Mr Jaskolski made the oral submissions that Ms Beiler was “alienating” the girls from their father (although it was denied that they had in fact expressed a reluctance to spend time with their father) and that Ms Beiler’s evidence regarding Y’s concerns about spending time with her father was “make believe”. This is a complex matter involving entrenched parental conflict which cannot be reduced to these kinds of terms.

For the reasons set out below, I am satisfied that Ms Beiler has contravened Orders 5(a) and 5(c) as alleged. I am not satisfied that she had a reasonable excuse for doing so. I require her to enter a bond with the condition that she comply with the orders in respect of Z for a period of two years. Ms Beiler remains obliged to comply with the parenting orders in respect of Y but, given Y’s age, I am not prepared to require Ms Beiler to enter a bond in respect of Y. I am not satisfied that it is in the children’s best interests to order “make up time”.

MATERIAL RELIED UPON

Mr Jaskolski relied on his affidavits filed 16 December 2025 and 2 April 2026. I received those affidavits into evidence with their annexures. Those annexures included, in relation to the previous proceedings, the Reasons for Judgment of Gill J dated 5 September 2025 (“Reasons for Judgment”), the Updated Family Report of Court Child Expert Ms C dated 11 December 2024 (“the Updated Family Report”), and the Child Impact Report of Court Child Expert Ms C dated 11 May 2023 (“the Child Impact Report”). Tendered in his case was an email exchange between the parents in the lead up to time occurring between 28 February and 10 March 2025.

Ms Beiler relied on her affidavit filed 27 March 2026, which I received into evidence with its annexure. She also relied on an affidavit of her partner, Mr R filed on 27 March 2026. Affidavits of Ms T and Ms M were not relied upon.

I have read and considered all the evidence, including the oral evidence which was given under cross examination by each of the parents and by Mr R.

Material had been produced by the Department of Communities and Justice and by NSW Police pursuant to an order under s 67ZBE of the Family Law Act 1975 (Cth) (“the Act”) – indeed, the hearing of the matter had been delayed to allow time for that material to be returned – but neither party tendered the material, and so I assume that it did not assist either party’s case.

Both parties filed Case Outlines. I have read and considered the written submissions contained in the Case Outline filed on behalf of Ms Beiler. I have read the Case Outline filed on behalf of Mr Jaskolski, which was settled by his counsel. The latter Case Outline contained a detailed chronology. The chronology contained no references to the evidential sources for the entries and contained many entries which were not supported by evidence that would be before me. I have ignored that chronology. I considered the closing oral submissions made by both counsel.

SOME BACKGROUND

Seven months ago, Gill J determined that it was in the interests of Y and Z to spend time with their father broadly in accordance with the proposals of their mother. That has never occurred. His Honour’s Reasons for Judgment were in evidence in these proceedings, and I am entitled to adopt any finding made by his Honour (s 102NN(3)(b) of the Act).

X’s move to live with Mr Jaskolski

X is now 16 and is not the subject of these contravention proceedings. The final orders provide that X is to live with Mr Jaskolski and spend time and communicate with Ms Beiler “in accordance with his wishes”. Mr Jaskolski contends that the background to that outcome is relevant to my assessment of Ms Beiler’s conduct in this matter.

In October 2024, X was 14 and a half. Justice Gill found at [183]–[184] that:

October 2024 marked a sharp breakdown of parenting arrangements, with [X]. On 12 October, shortly before the commencement of the trial on 21 October, [X] refused to return from the care of the husband to the wife’s care. It appears that [X] had planned to remain with the husband following the holiday as he had discreetly removed some of his belonging and taken them with him to his father’s house.

184 The wife sought a recovery order, which was opposed by the husband.

His Honour continued at [199]–[201]:

In response to the wife’s application, on 22 October 2024 I caused [X] to attend upon a Court Child Expert, to whom [X] expressed strongly that he would not return to his mother, and expressed that he did not feel safe to return to her.

In response to the interim application for the return of [X], on 23 October 2024 I made interim orders that provided for [X] to live with the husband ...

The husband was due to spend time with [Y] and [Z] on 15 November 2024. The wife messaged the husband the day prior, saying that the girls would not be seeing him. The wife explained the withholding to the report writer on the basis that she “lost confidence in both the existing Court orders and the willingness to enforce them.”

Other relevant findings of Gill J

Thus, Ms Beiler did not provide Y or Z for ordered time with their father in November 2024.

I adopt the following further relevant findings:

At [206]–[207] of the Reasons for Judgment:

The husband was due to collect the girls on 10 January 2025. The wife and [Mr R] attended with the girls for handover, but without any luggage for the girls. [Y] said to the husband that she and [Z] wanted to return early for [Z’s] birthday. The wife and [Mr R] intervened, effectively insisting that the husband there and then accede to the request for an early return (on the basis that this accorded with the wife’s understanding of what the girls wanted). A heated argument took place in the presence of the children between the adults.

207 The girls did not go with the husband. ...

On this occasion, “the wife was undermining the girls’ time with the husband, in a manner inconsistent with her obligations pursuant to the orders” (at [207] of the Reasons for Judgment).

This occurred after the release of the Family Report, which had “cautioned against the wife preventing the girls spending time with the husband” (at [208] of the Reasons for Judgment).

“... While in this instance there was not a direct withholding, the wife’s failure to encourage and support the girls’ time with X and the husband in accordance with the orders failed to take heed of the caution expressed by the report writer” (Reasons for Judgment at [208]).

In relation to Y’s and Z’s views, his Honour found at [215]–[216] that:

[Y] and [Z] expressed highly positive views of the wife, consistent with both a more positive relationship with the wife [than [X]], but also consistent with each harbouring concerns as to the wife’s reaction.

[Y] and [Z] expressed strong views across all of the assessments that they want to live with the wife ...

While his Honour did not make any findings about Y’s and Z’s views in relation to spending time with Mr Jaskolski (presumably because both parents were seeking at least that this occur), the Updated Family Report records that both wanted to spend time with Mr Jaskolski in accordance with the arrangements then in place (which were broadly the same as those ultimately ordered).

The events of March 2025

On behalf of Ms Beiler, it is said that the events of early March 2025 and the behaviour to which the children were exposed are the context in which the children will not spend time with Mr Jaskolski. Ms Beiler gave evidence that those events “caused me serious concern for [Y’s and Z’s] emotional and physical safety”.

In March 2025, judgment was reserved. The proceedings were not re-opened. There are therefore no relevant findings of Gill J in relation to those events.

Mr Jaskolski’s first affidavit (filed with the first contravention application) made no reference to those events. Ms Beiler’s affidavit dealt in detail with those events. In his affidavit in reply, Mr Jaskolski then gave evidence about what he says occurred.

I accept the submission of counsel for Ms Beiler that the events of March 2025 do neither party any credit and involved a significant lack of child focus by both parents. The events involved a dispute over a horse (“[Horse 33]”) which Mr Jaskolski had brought with him, along with another horse, when he travelled with X to Y’s and Z’s hometown to spend time with them there for 10 days during the school term.

The accounts given by the parents and Mr R differ in significant respects and I am able and, for the purposes of this application, need only to make very limited findings. Horse 33 had been given by Mr Jaskolski to X, and I accept Mr Jaskolski’s evidence that X rode Horse 33 daily and loved his horse. On 1 March 2025, being the second day of Y’s and Z’s time with Mr Jaskolski, Horse 33 and the other horse escaped and entered Ms Beiler’s property. At the time, Mr Jaskolski and X apparently believed that Ms Beiler was deliberately withholding Horse 33. Mr Jaskolski undertook various unhelpful and public steps (in a small community) to try and recover Horse 33. X was very distressed at the loss of his horse. Mr Jaskolski drove X to Ms Beiler’s property so that he could speak with his mother and try to recover Horse 33. Y and Z remained at the house where they were staying with Mr Jaskolski. Ms Beiler’s parents, but not Ms Beiler and Mr R, were at Ms Beiler’s residence. There was an encounter between X and the maternal grandparents which I accept likely involved X using foul language towards them. Ms Beiler did not return X’s horse, and she gave evidence that Horse 33 remains on her property and that she is not prepared to return Horse 33. She was not asked why. The other horse was returned.

It is not contested that, in a subsequent telephone call to his mother, X used foul language in the face of her refusal to return Horse 33. It is not contested that the police were telephoned following X’s interaction with his grandparents, and that the police attended the house where Mr Jaskolski and X were staying with Y and Z, and spoke with X. The police took no further action against X. Mr Jaskolski provided an audio recording of X using foul language to another person in the community, who played the recording to Ms Beiler. Mr Jaskolski says he regrets doing so, but was trying to recover the horse for X, who was in great distress.

So far as what occurred for Y and Z at this time, the parents’ accounts differ profoundly. At the time of the March 2025 events, the girls were staying with their father and X. In summary, Mr Jaskolski gives evidence that in relation to the March 2025 events, the girls were concerned for X and his obvious distress in relation to Horse 33, were distressed themselves and sought to comfort X. Mr Jaskolski deposes to happy times with the girls and X at the Town G show and with Z at the park. He denies the numerous accusations that Ms Beiler makes about that time – including that he removed Y’s phone, that X was swearing and punching walls and a range of other things.

Ms Beiler’s evidence about what she says the girls experienced while in the care of Mr Jaskolski and with X is of poor quality, in that it does not particularise how she is aware of this information, notwithstanding that for most of this time, she was not present. She refers vaguely to having been told about Mr Jaskolski’s behaviour during this period by a neighbour, by Mr R or by Y, but provides no details of any such conversations. I attach no weight to that evidence and, appropriately, Mr Jaskolski was not cross examined about it.

Under cross examination, Ms Beiler accepted that she had received a text message from Z which read “please return the horse to [X]”. That provides some corroboration of Mr Jaskolski’s evidence that the girls were concerned for X. Ms Beiler was evasive about the circumstances in which X used Y’s phone to ask for the return of his horse, refuting the suggestion that Y had allowed her brother to use her phone and instead claiming “she phoned me and he took the phone from her”. She also could not simply accept that X had asked for his horse back, but rather chose to focus on telling me that X “swore and yelled”. Clearly X was asking for his horse back and her refusal to accept that does her no credit.

Notwithstanding what occurred, and that they were in their hometown and very close to their home (and so could easily have returned), Y and Z spent time with Mr Jaskolski for the two weekends and the school week provided for under the final orders, and Mr Jaskolski took them to extra-curricular activities. That is more consistent with Mr Jaskolski’s evidence than the evidence of Ms Beiler.

The events of the period from 1 March 2025 were no doubt distressing for the girls. Those distressing events were caused by both parents in the context of the ongoing conflict and poor child focus. I am not persuaded that there was anything in what occurred which gave Ms Beiler any reasonable basis for concern about the girls’ safety in the care of Mr Jaskolski.

COUNTS 5 AND 6 – WITHHOLDING ON 4 OCTOBER 2025

Both counts relate to the events of 4 October 2025, when Y and Z were to be taken to changeover at a point approximately halfway between the parents’ respective homes, to spend school holiday time with their father. The contraventions are pleaded in two different ways. Count 5 alleges that Ms Beiler contravened Order 5(c) in that she “without reasonable excuse withheld the children from spending holiday time with [Mr Jaskolski]”. Count 6 alleges that Ms Beiler contravened Order 5(d) (which specifies the handover location), in that she “without reasonable excuse failed to produce/deliver the children at/to changeover at the commencement of holiday time with [Mr Jaskolski]”. This Count is misconceived in that the obligation is created by Order 5(c) (together with Order 5(b)), rather than 5(d) (which is mechanical only). I therefore deal only with Count 5.

Did Ms Beiler contravene Order 5(c)?

A person contravenes a child-related order only if that person “intentionally fails to comply with the order” or “makes no reasonable attempt to comply with the order” (s 70NAC(1)(a)). I must be satisfied on the balance of probabilities that the contravention has occurred, and the onus lies on Mr Jaskolski.

I am comfortably satisfied that Ms Beiler intentionally failed to comply with Order 5(c) on 4 October by withholding Y and Z, and that therefore she contravened that order. This is not a case where Ms Beiler tried to comply with the order but was unable to – as was implied by some of the submissions made on her behalf before the evidence was heard. She simply decided not to comply in circumstances where she says Y and Z were telling her they did not want to go.

That this is the case is made clear by the following:

(1) Ms Beiler instructed her lawyers to inform Mr Jaskolski via his lawyers on 3 October 2025 (the day before time was to occur) that she would not be making the children available for time with their father. Mr Jaskolski’s lawyers responded on the same day, advising that he would be at the changeover point at the appointed time. Mr Jaskolski attended the changeover location on 4 October, and sent a message to Ms Beiler when he arrived, asking how far away she was. Ms Beiler did not trouble to reply.

(2) Ms Beiler and Mr R gave evidence under cross examination that, on that day, they were watching a horse show with the children at Town U. Mr R agreed with the proposition put to him by counsel for Mr Jaskolski that their attendance at the show had been planned “for some time”.

(3) Ms Beiler gave no evidence that she had attempted on 4 October to get the children into the car, nor indeed that she had taken any step at all on that day to try to persuade them to attend.

Ms Beiler intentionally failed to comply with Order 5(c) on 4 October 2025.

Did Ms Beiler have a reasonable excuse for contravening the orders on 4 October 2025?

Ms Beiler bears the onus of persuading me that she has a reasonable excuse for contravening this order (s 70NADA of the Act). Sub-section 70NAD(3) sets out when a person has a reasonable excuse for contravening a child-related order:

Protection of health or safety of a person

(3) A person has a reasonable excuse for contravening a child‑related order if:

(a) the person contravened the order because the person reasonably believed that the person’s actions constituting the contravention were necessary to protect the health or safety of the person, a child or any other person; and

(b) the period of the contravention was not longer than necessary to protect the health or safety of the person, child or other person.

The section does not limit the circumstances in which a person may have a reasonable excuse for contravening a child-related order (s 70NAD(4)).

In the Case Outline filed on Ms Beiler’s behalf, the submission was made at paragraph 41 that she “says that she has a reasonable excuse in that she ought to be excused and there was a serious threat to the health and safety of the children, where she gives evidence of threats to ‘run away’ which had force given their ages”.

The relevant evidence given by Ms Beiler was as follows:

(1) “[Y] told me that if she and [Z] were made to travel to [City E] then at the first available opportunity she would take [Z] and hide in the bush until I could come and collect them”;

(2) “After discussing the situation with [Y] at length, she remained adamant that she did not want to go and that if she was made to go she would run away”;

(3) “... both girls were adamant that they would not go to [City E] to spend time with [Mr Jaskolski] and [Y] was adamant that if attempts were made to force them to do so they would run away”;

(4) Ms Beiler conceded under cross examination that it was “not necessarily [Z]” who had raised the prospect of running away, but rather “mostly [Y]”.

It can be immediately observed that:

(1) There was no context whatsoever provided for these conversations, and very limited detail of any conversation between Ms Beiler and either or both of her daughters.

(2) There was also no evidence that Z had threatened to run away.

I am not satisfied based on this evidence that either of the girls threatened to run away, nor that Ms Beiler genuinely believed that they would. I reiterate that I am also not satisfied that there was anything in the events of the week commencing 1 March 2025 which gave Ms Beiler a reasonable basis to be concerned for the safety of the Y and Z in the care of Mr Jaskolski.

To the extent that the reasonable excuse defence relies on what it is said are Y’s and Z’s views more generally, I find and determine as follows:

(1) There is no reliable evidence which enables me to conclude what those views are:

(a) If Y and Z do not want to spend time with Mr Jaskolski, that would be a very significant change from their views expressed to the Family Report writer (albeit in November 2024).

(b) Again, Ms Beiler’s affidavit evidence is insufficiently particularised.

(c) Again, there is no evidence of any weight about Z’s views.

(d) Mr Jaskolski’s evidence, while also unsatisfactory, was to the effect that the girls tell him they want to spend time with him.

(2) In any case, it is not a reasonable excuse for intentionally not complying with Court orders that children do not want to spend time with their parent in accordance with those orders.
